title: [MEDIUM] Bound and correctly account for MCP JSON-RPC batches
description: Deep sweep finding: McpAdapter.handle applies the rate limiter once to an entire JSON-RPC batch, then processes every request concurrently with Promise.all. A large batch can bypass RATE_LIMIT_MAX_REQUESTS, consume memory/CPU, and run state-mutating tools concurrently even when individual tools do not all take StateManager's mutex. WebSocketServer.trackMcpWorker also ignores batch requests, so wait/chat waiters from batched calls are not tracked for disconnect cleanup. Fix batch handling with explicit size limits, per-request accounting, worker tracking, and safe execution semantics.
definitionOfDone: MCP JSON-RPC batch size is capped with a configurable safe default; oversized batches return JSON-RPC errors without executing all items.
Rate limiting is charged per request in a batch, not once per array.
Batch workerId tracking covers each tools/call item so disconnect cleanup cancels wait_for_task/chat_wait waiters created by batched calls.
State-mutating tool calls in a batch are serialized or otherwise protected so concurrent batch execution cannot corrupt task/worker state.
Regression tests cover oversized batch rejection, per-request rate limiting, batch worker tracking, and mixed read/write batch behavior.

implementationPlan.description: Add MAX_MCP_BATCH_SIZE configuration with a conservative default. In McpAdapter.handle, validate arrays before execution; for oversized batches, return error handling responses per request id where possible (or a single parse-level error if shape is invalid) without executing tool handlers.
implementationPlan.note: Added MOE_MCP_MAX_BATCH_SIZE parsing with conservative default 25 and early batch validation before execution. Oversized batches now return a compact single JSON-RPC error with no payload echo and no tool execution; empty batches get invalid-request. Added regression test and verified it failed before the fix (old behavior executed as an array), then targeted McpAdapter batch tests pass 3/3.

implementationPlan.description: Move rate-limit checks to per-request accounting. For arrays, apply the limiter to each request and return rate-limit errors for requests over quota. Preserve existing single-request behavior and add regression test coverage.
implementationPlan.note: Moved rate limiting from one top-level check per handle() call to handleSingleWithRateLimit(), so each batch item is charged independently and later items get compact rate-limit errors over quota while earlier responses remain in order. TDD red: batch of 4 with RateLimiter(2) previously returned success for item 3; green targeted McpAdapter batch/rate tests pass 4/4.

implementationPlan.description: Change batch execution to safe sequencing for tools/call or add a mutating-tool guard so state-changing operations cannot run concurrently. Keep read-only lifecycle requests efficient if desired, but prefer correctness over parallelism.
implementationPlan.note: Changed batch execution from Promise.all to ordered sequential handling, preserving response order while preventing concurrent tools/call handlers from mutating shared state simultaneously. TDD red: a detector tool observed concurrent in-flight calls under old Promise.all; green targeted McpAdapter batch tests pass 5/5.

implementationPlan.description: Update WebSocketServer.trackMcpWorker to iterate batch arrays and extract workerId from each tools/call item. Add disconnect-cleanup tests for batched wait/chat worker IDs and run daemon npm test/build.
implementationPlan.note: Updated WebSocketServer.trackMcpWorker to accept unknown/batch requests and inspect every batch item for tools/call arguments.workerId, with malformed entries ignored safely. Added disconnect-cleanup regression coverage proving batched wait_for_task/chat_wait worker IDs are tracked and cleanupMcpWorkers cancels both active waiters. Adversarial review also added malformed batch-member handling in McpAdapter so null/invalid items return compact -32600 errors and do not drop later responses. Red checks observed failures before fixes for oversized batch, per-request rate accounting, concurrent batch tools/call, malformed batch member, and batch worker tracking. Final verification: targeted McpAdapter+WebSocketServer tests pass 35/35; npm run build exit 0; full npm test -- --reporter=dot exit 0 with 25 files / 474 tests; git diff --check clean except CRLF warnings.

reopenReason: Rejected: actual /mcp batch disconnect cleanup is incomplete. Targeted batch/WebSocket tests pass, but an integration repro still leaks a waiter: a batch with wait_for_task followed by chat_wait, then socket close while wait_for_task is pending, cancels the first waiter but then starts chat_wait after cleanup removed connection tracking. This fails the disconnect cleanup DoD and needs a real WebSocket regression test.

comments.author: architect-2992
comments.content: Token-budget check: oversized/invalid JSON-RPC batch errors must be compact and must not echo the full batch. Cap batch size and charge rate limits per item.

planningNotes.approachesConsidered: Rejected simply disabling all batch support because JSON-RPC allows it and clients may use it for tools/list/ping. Prefer bounded batches and sequential execution for tools/call, or an explicit mutating-tool classification if parallel reads are retained.
planningNotes.codebaseInsights: McpAdapter currently uses Promise.all for arrays and has no concept of read vs write tools. Some mutating tools are internally mutexed, but many rely on caller-side serialization. WebSocketServer.trackMcpWorker sees only top-level request.method.
planningNotes.risks: Need preserve notifications with no response and mixed batch response ordering if tests expect JSON-RPC behavior. Avoid introducing deadlocks by wrapping tools that already use StateManager.runExclusive incorrectly. Include error handling and test coverage for invalid batch members.

failedDodItems: Batch workerId tracking covers each tools/call item so disconnect cleanup cancels wait_for_task/chat_wait waiters created by batched calls.
Regression tests cover oversized batch rejection, per-request rate limiting, batch worker tracking, and mixed read/write batch behavior.

issues.description: Batch processing continues after the MCP socket closes. Repro: /mcp batch wait_for_task then chat_wait; close while wait_for_task is active. Cleanup cancels wait_for_task and deletes connection tracking, then the loop starts chat_wait after close, leaving activeChatWaiters until timeout. Abort remaining batch work on close or keep cleanup tied to later waiter creation.

The batch worker cleanup test only calls private track/cleanup helpers with pre-populated waiters. It misses actual /mcp sequencing. Add an integration test that sends a real WebSocket batch, disconnects mid-batch while the first long-poll is pending, and asserts no activeWaiters/activeChatWaiters remain.
